lead.title: Is Berlin Walkable? How Far Apart the Main Sights Really Are (2026)
lead.excerpt: Is Berlin walkable? The central sights are far closer than the map suggests, and flat underfoot. Here is how far apart they really are, the one walk to do on foot, and when to take the train instead.
lead.alt: The Bode Museum dome and the TV Tower rising across the Spree on Berlin's Museum Island, two of the central sights that sit within an easy walk of each other

secondary.title: Berlin Ghost Stations: The 15 Stops Trains Passed Without Stopping

shelves.title: Tour Route Stories
shelves.description: The places around the BerlinWalk route, explained as story layers rather than postcard stops.

posts.title: 12 Stops Through Berlin’s Ancient Core: What You’ll See on Our Free Walking Tour
posts.excerpt: Most Berlin free walking tours start at the Brandenburg Gate and head west. Ours doesn't. We begin where Berlin itself began — at Alexanderplatz — and walk through the oldest neighborhoods in the city, ending at the vibrant Hackescher Markt. Over 12 carefully chosen stops, you'll trace 800 years of history through streets that most tourists never explore. This isn't a greatest-hits tour of famous landmarks. It's a walk through the layers of Berlin — from its medieval founding to Prussian...
posts.alt: Illustration of Berlin landmarks in green and yellow. Features buildings like the Berlin TV Tower, Brandenburg Gate, and Berlin Cathedral.

posts.title: 7 Things Most Tourists Don't Know About the Berliner Dom

posts.title: Berlin in October 2026: Weather, Festival of Lights, Packing and What to Book
posts.excerpt: October is the month I send people to when they want Berlin without the crowds, but still want the city to feel alive. The summer heat is gone. The tourist peak is over. But the parks are still beautiful, the museums are comfortable, and the autumn light gives the whole city a softer, more cinematic mood. For a few weeks, Berlin turns gold. Visiting Berlin in October means accepting that the weather is a real variable and the evenings get dark early. It also means getting some of the best...

latest.title: Stolpersteine in Berlin: How to Read the Brass Stones Under Your Feet (2026)
latest.excerpt: Berlin’s pavements hold more than 11,000 small brass memorials, and most visitors walk over them without seeing one. The text on every stone follows the same compact formula. Learn it once, in about ten minutes, and the city never looks the same.
latest.alt: Four brass Stolpersteine for the Salinger family set into dark cobblestones in a Berlin pavement

latest.title: Schöneberg Berlin: Bowie, Kennedy and the District That Whispers (2026)
